}

messages.content:
You are a question answering assistant. Your job is to generate a SPARQL query to answer a given user question.

You should follow a step-by-step approach to generate the SPARQL query:
1. Determine possible entities and properties implied by the user question.
2. Search for the entities and properties in the knowledge graphs. Where applicable, constrain the searches with already identified entities and properties.
3. Gradually build up the SPARQL query using the identified entities and properties. Start with simple queries and add more complexity as needed. Execute intermediate queries to get feedback and to verify your assumptions. You may need to refine or rethink your current plan based on the query results and go back to step 2 if needed, possibly multiple times.
4. Use the answer or cancel function to finalize your answer and stop the generation process.

Available knowledge graphs:
- "wikidata" at https://qlever.dev/api/wikidata
  - "entity" index (type="fuzzy"): Entities indexed by their labels and synonyms
  - "property" index (type="embedding", modalities="text"): Properties indexed by their labels, synonyms, and identifiers

Index types used:
- "embedding": Retrieves items by cosine similarity between their embeddings and the query embedding. The embedding model used depends on the index and may support text, images, or both.
- "fuzzy": Retrieves items by overlap between their label words and the query keywords. The query keywords must not match label words exactly, but some fuzziness is allowed. The longer a query keyword is, the more it can deviate from a label word and still be considered a match, though it will also contribute less to the overall score. No special query operators like AND/OR are supported.

SPARQL prefixes for use in function calls:
- bd: http://www.bigdata.com/rdf#
- cc: http://creativecommons.org/ns#
- data: https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Special:EntityData/
- dc: http://purl.org/dc/elements/1.1/
- dct: http://purl.org/dc/terms/
- foaf: http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/
- geo: http://www.opengis.net/ont/geosparql#
- geof: http://www.opengis.net/def/function/geosparql/
- gn: http://www.geonames.org/ontology#
- hint: http://www.bigdata.com/queryHints#
- imdb: https://www.imdb.com/
- math: http://www.w3.org/2005/xpath-functions/math#
- ontolex: http://www.w3.org/ns/lemon/ontolex#
- owl: http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#
- p: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/
- pq: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/qualifier/
- pqn: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/qualifier/value-normalized/
- pqv: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/qualifier/value/
- pr: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/reference/
- prn: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/reference/value-normalized/
- prov: http://www.w3.org/ns/prov#
- prv: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/reference/value/
- ps: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/statement/
- psn: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/statement/value-normalized/
- psv: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/statement/value/
- qb: http://purl.org/linked-data/cube#
- qfn: http://qlever.cs.uni-freiburg.de/function#
- ql: http://qlever.cs.uni-freiburg.de/builtin-functions/
- qlss: https://qlever.cs.uni-freiburg.de/spatialSearch/
- rdf: http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#
- rdfs: http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#
- ref: http://www.wikidata.org/reference/
- s: http://www.wikidata.org/entity/statement/
- schema: http://schema.org/
- skos: http://www.w3.org/2004/02/skos/core#
- v: http://www.wikidata.org/value/
- void: http://rdfs.org/ns/void#
- wd: http://www.wikidata.org/entity/
- wdno: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/novalue/
- wdt: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/direct/
- wdtn: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/direct-normalized/
- wikibase: http://wikiba.se/ontology#
- xsd: http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#

Additional rules to follow:
- Explain your thought process before and after each step and function call.
- Do not ask the user for clarification until you have made an initial attempt at completing the task. When the task input is incomplete or ambiguous, proceed based on reasonable assumptions.
- Do not use "SERVICE wikibase:label { bd:serviceParam wikibase:language ..." in SPARQL queries. It is unsupported by the used QLever SPARQL endpoints. Use rdfs:label or similar properties to get labels instead.
- Always execute your final SPARQL query before giving an answer to make sure it returns the expected results.
- The SPARQL query should always return the actual identifiers / IRIs of the items in its result. It additionally may return labels or other human-readable information, but they are optional and should be put within optional clauses unless explicitly requested by the user.
- Do not stop early if there are still obvious improvements to be made to the SPARQL query. For example, keep refining your SPARQL query if its result contains irrelevant items or is missing items you expected.
- Do not perform additional computation (e.g. filtering, sorting, calculations) on the result of the SPARQL query to determine the answer. All computation should be done solely within SPARQL.
- For questions with a "True" or "False" answer the SPARQL query should be an ASK query.

tool_calls.name: search_property
tool_calls.result:
Top 10 property alternatives:
1. present in work (wd:P1441 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "character in"), also known as "appeared in", "appears in", "appears in work", "character in", "featured in work", etc.:
  - this (fictional or fictionalized) entity, place, or person ... P1343 for entities described in non-fictional accounts)
2. opposite of (wd:P461 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "antagonist"), also known as "adversary", "antagonist", "contrasted to", "contrast of", "has opposite", etc.:
  - item that is in some way the opposite of this item
3. character role (wd:P453 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv), also known as "acting role", "as", "performer of", "plays", "role":
  - specific role played or filled by subject -- use only as qualifier of "cast member" (P161), "voice actor" (P725)
4. significant person (wd:P3342 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "key person"), also known as "associated people", "associated person", "employee", "friend", "friends", etc.:
  - person linked to the item in any possible way
5. main subject (wd:P921 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "main thing"), also known as "about", "artistic theme", "/common/topic/subject", "content deals with", "content describes", etc.:
  - primary topic of a work or act of communication
6. cast member (wd:P161 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "actor"), also known as "actor", "actress", "contestant of a play", "feat.", "film starring", etc.:
  - actor in the subject production [use "character role" (P453) ... "recorded participant" (P11108) for non-fiction productions]
7. performer (wd:P175 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "actor"), also known as "actor", "actress", "artist", "dancer", "musical artist", etc.:
  - actor, musician, band or other performer associated with this role or musical work
8. agent of action (wd:P12995 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "actor"), also known as "actor", "agent", "being done by", "did by", "doer", etc.:
  - particular animate item that initiates this action or class of actions
9. significant event (wd:P793 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "main events"), also known as "big event", "event", "fate", "important event", "key event", etc.:
  - significant or notable events associated with the subject
10. antagonist of (wd:P3773 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv):
  - protein for which this chemical compound acts as an antagonist

reasoning.content:
Okay, let's tackle the user's question: "Who is the main character in the film Shawshank Redemption?"

First, I need to figure out which entity represents the film. From the previous search_entity call, the top result was "The Shawshank Redemption" with the identifier wd:Q172241. That's the film entry on Wikidata. Good, so the film's IRI is wd:Q172241.

Next, I need to find the main character. The user is asking for the main character in the film, so I should look for a property that links the film to its main character. In the search_property call for "main character", the results included "present in work" (wdt:P1441), "character role" (wdt:P453), and "cast member" (wdt:P161). 

Wait, "character role" (P453) is a qualifier used with "cast member" (P161). So the typical pattern would be that the film has a cast member (P161), and that cast member has a character role (P453). However, the question is about the main character, so maybe we need to find the character played by the lead actor.

Looking at the film's entry in Wikidata, the main character is Andy Dufresne, played by Tim Robbins. But how to get this from Wikidata?

Let me think about the properties. The property "cast member" (P161) would list all actors in the film. Then, each cast member entry might have a qualifier "character role" (P453) specifying which character they played. So if I can find the cast member with the lead role, their character role would be the main character.

Alternatively, sometimes there's a property like "main character" directly, but searching for "main character" gave "present in work" (P1441) which is for fictional entities. Wait, P1441 is "present in work", which is used to indicate a fictional character appearing in a work. So if Andy Dufresne is the main character, there might be an entry where the film (P1441) is The Shawshank Redemption, and the entity is Andy Dufresne.

Looking at the search_property results, the first one was P1441 "present in work", which is described as "this (fictional or fictionalized) entity, place, or person ...". So if I use P1441, the object would be the film, and the subject would be the character.

Wait, the direction matters. The property "present in work" (P1441) is used as: [Character] P1441 [Film]. So to find the character, we need to look for entities that have P1441 pointing to the film.

So the query would be: Find ?character where ?character wdt:P1441 wd:Q172241.

But let me check if that's correct. For example, in Wikidata, does Andy Dufresne have the "present in work" property set to Shawshank Redemption?

Yes, checking manually (though I can't actually do that here), but based on Wikidata structure. So the correct approach is to find entities with P1441 equal to the film.

Alternatively, sometimes the main role might be specified using a different property, but given the search_property results, P1441 seems like the right candidate.

Let me try to construct the SPARQL query.

First, get the film entity: wd:Q172241.

Then, look for entities that have wdt:P1441 equal to wd:Q172241. Those entities should be the characters in the film.

But which one is the main character? Probably the one with the most prominence, like Andy Dufresne.

In the search_property results, there's also "main subject" (P921), but that's more for topics of a work, like themes. Not exactly characters.

Another property might be "narrative location" but that's a stretch.

Alternatively, maybe the lead actor's character can be inferred by checking who has the character role associated with the lead actor.

Wait, the cast member property (P161) lists actors. If I get the actors and their character roles (using P453 as a qualifier), then the lead actor (maybe Tim Robbins) would have the main character (Andy Dufresne) as his character role.

So the steps could be:

1. Find the film's cast members (P161).
2. For each cast member, get their character role (P453).
3. Determine which character is the main one, perhaps by checking if the actor is the lead.

But how to determine the lead actor? Maybe the order isn't specified in Wikidata. Alternatively, the main character might be explicitly linked via P1441.

Given that P1441 is "present in work", which includes fictional characters, the main character should be listed there.

So the SPARQL query would be:

SELECT ?character ?characterLabel WHERE {
  ?character wdt:P1441 wd:Q172241 .
  SERVICE wikibase:label { bd:serviceParam wikibase:language "en" }
}

Wait, but the user mentioned not to use SERVICE wikibase:label because it's unsupported. Oh, right, the note says: Do not use "SERVICE wikibase:label { bd:serviceParam wikibase:language ..." in SPARQL queries. It is unsupported by the used QLever SPARQL endpoints. Use rdfs:label or similar properties to get labels instead.

So instead, use the optional clause with rdfs:label.

WHERE {

  OPTIONAL { ?character rdfs:label ?characterLabel FILTER (LANG(?characterLabel) = "en") }
